Ayah 2 · linked video: Al-Jumu’ah (Ayah 2)

Surah Al-Jumuah, Ayah 2

هُوَ الَّذِي بَعَثَ فِي الْأُمِّيِّينَ رَسُولًا مِّنْهُمْ يَتْلُو عَلَيْهِمْ آيَاتِهِ وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحِكْمَةَ وَإِن كَانُوا مِن قَبْلُ لَفِي ضَلَالٍ مُّبِينٍ

He is the One who raised among the unlettered a Messenger from among themselves—reciting to them His verses, purifying them, and teaching them the Book and wisdom, although they were previously in clear misguidance.

This ayah holds a unique place among the Musabbihat (surahs beginning with the glorification of Allah ﷻ). In many of these surahs, Allah ﷻ begins by mentioning the universe and His dominion, then addresses humanity with criticism or demands they’re failing to fulfill. In Surah Al-Hadid, for instance, six ayat describe Allah’s names, attributes and kingdom, followed by pointed questions: What’s wrong with you? Why aren’t you doing what you’re supposed to do? Why do you say what you don’t do?

But Surah Al-Jumuah follows a different pattern. Immediately after mentioning Himself, Allah ﷻ mentions His Messenger ﷺ. This structure is significant, and the ayah itself carries profound implications about the Prophet’s mission ﷺ and methodology.

The Unlettered Prophet ﷺ

The ayah begins: “He is the One who appointed, raised from among those who cannot read—unlettered.” The word ummi (أُمِّيِّين) derives from umm (mother). An ummi person has received about as much formal education as they had when they emerged from their mother’s womb. The term literally means someone who cannot read or write, someone whose formal education is equivalent to that of a newborn.

Allah ﷻ says He raised “among the ummiyeen” (the unlettered people) “rasulan minhum”—a messenger belonging to them, a messenger from among themselves.

This detail becomes particularly significant when we understand one of the audiences of this surah: the Jews of Madinah. The sacred day of the Jews was Saturday, the Sabbath, and Allah ﷻ had now established Friday as the sacred day for the Muslim community. This was a Madani surah, and the Jewish community was listening. One of their primary criticisms of the Prophet ﷺ was precisely this: that he came from among the ummiyeen, the unlettered Arabs.

Allah ﷻ transforms their insult into an honor by making it part of the revelation itself. Yes, He appointed a messenger among the ummiyeen, from among them. This is not a flaw but a feature of divine wisdom.

The Miraculous Nature of Recitation

What does this unlettered Prophet ﷺ do? The ayah tells us: yatlu ’alayhim ayatih—“he recites to them His ayat.” If he’s unlettered and cannot read, how is he reading? And notice the word choice: yatlu (يَتْلُو), which means to read or recite. The word inherently implies a miracle—that despite being unlettered, he was reciting the Quran to people.

But there’s something deeper here. Reading is fundamentally different from speaking. If you hand someone a text and ask them to read it aloud, even listeners who cannot see the reader can tell they’re reading rather than speaking. There’s a clear distinction. Especially when you know someone well, you can immediately recognize when they’re speaking their own words versus when they’re reading someone else’s composition. The author’s speech patterns and style of composition become evident in the reading.

The Prophet ﷺ had been speaking to the Makkans for forty years. His companions knew his manner of speech intimately. But when revelation came and he began reciting the Quran, everyone could tell: this is different. Where did that come from? He never talks like that. What he was saying was clearly not his own words.

This distinction exists even beyond the theological understanding of revelation. From a purely observational standpoint, anyone listening could recognize that this speech pattern, this composition, this vocabulary was fundamentally different from the Prophet’s normal discourse.

Reading from the Heart

There’s another dimension to consider. Allah ﷻ tells us that Jibreel (AS), the Trusted Spirit, brought down the Quran “onto your heart” (نَزَلَ بِهِ عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ). The Quran was revealed onto the heart of the Prophet ﷺ. You and I read with our eyes from a page. The implication is that the Prophet ﷺ reads from his heart—it’s revealed onto his heart.

Even in the first revelation, the Prophet ﷺ wasn’t shown a piece of paper or a scroll and told to read. He was grabbed, shaken and commanded to read. So from where was he reading? This indicates something beyond ordinary reading—a reading from revelation received directly in the heart.

Yet even then, anyone listening could clearly recognize: this is not his speech, not his speech pattern.

The Linguistic Distinction

A fascinating area of study involves the linguistic comparison between Hadith and Quran. They’re completely different. The syntax differs, the structures differ, the vocabulary is worlds apart. A substantial portion of the vocabulary used in Hadith simply doesn’t appear in the Quran. Hadith is eloquent in its own right, beautiful and profound, and it’s part of revelation too. But Quran has its own unique revelation, its own syntax. It is Allah’s speech ﷻ.

The key to understanding this difference: Quran is the word of Allah ﷻ literally. Words like yusabbihu (يُسَبِّحُ) came directly from Allah ﷻ. Hadith contains divine teaching and wisdom, but the words themselves were chosen by the Prophet ﷺ. It’s inspired revelation, divine wisdom and guidance, but the Prophet ﷺ expressed it in his own words. The words are the Prophet’s, though the teachings are from Allah ﷻ.

This distinction even applies to Hadith Qudsi. In Quran, the Messenger ﷺ is quoting Allah ﷻ. In Hadith Qudsi, the Prophet ﷺ is paraphrasing Allah ﷻ, summarizing divine communication in his own expressions.

The Four-Stage Methodology

By mentioning the recitation of the Quran at the beginning, Allah ﷻ establishes the foundation of the Prophet’s mission ﷺ. The ayah then outlines a complete methodology for transformation:

Stage One: Intellectual Purification

Wa yuzakkihim (وَيُزَكِّيهِمْ)—“and he purifies them.” This purification operates on multiple levels. First, there’s intellectual cleansing—developing a sense of proportion, understanding the ethical foundations of Islam, recognizing that justice in our deen and doing right by people is a major priority.

Travel through the Muslim world today, and you’ll find that religiously conscious Muslims often display strong legal awareness (halal-haram consciousness) but weak social consciousness regarding justice and the rights of others. When it comes to halal meat, it’s a big priority. But compare how much the Quran emphasizes doing right by others versus how much it discusses dietary restrictions. Both are important, but we’ve emphasized one and de-emphasized the other. The Quran itself provides a balance, a proportion that allows for proper perspective.

Intellectual Detoxification

We live in a world of competing philosophies. College students completing just two years of study have been exposed to diverse worldviews and often face pressure—almost bullying—toward agnosticism. You’re better off believing nothing. You’re better off living with uncertainty. I’m not sure there are other ways of looking at it.

The Quran responds to intellectually hollow philosophies. It contains answers to these challenges. This isn’t merely theoretical—many people experience it personally. After exposure to too many philosophy courses (like consuming too much junk food), you need a detox.

The transformative power of engaging with the entire Quran as conversation rather than mere recitation can be life-changing. When you’re genuinely listening and paying attention, you forget there’s someone standing in front of you. It becomes just you and the Quran. You don’t feel like someone’s talking to you—you feel Allah ﷻ is talking directly to you.

This intellectual purification addresses the spiritual void many feel. Human beings were created with a space in their hearts that can only be filled with revelation. When that space remains unfilled, people try to fill it with movies, music, video games, addictions—something else. In religious communities where Quran becomes a distant scholarly pursuit rather than every Muslim’s intimate connection, people seek spiritual highs through other means.

Stage Two: Spiritual Purification

The purification (tazkiyah) includes spiritual cleansing. This means addressing anger problems, jealousy, laziness, character flaws. The Quran begins working on these character defects. It addresses ghaflah (heedlessness) and heartless prayer. When you start paying attention to the Quran and it’s recited to you repeatedly, you begin becoming conscious. You cannot ignore it indefinitely. It continues reaching you, and though it may not impact you every single time, the frequency of those moments when it does impact you increases because you’re exposed to Quran more. There are more occasions when you’re moved to tears, not fewer. It keeps purifying.

The highest form of spirituality given to the Prophet ﷺ occurred when he reached the highest place any human being has ever reached—by the Throne of Allah ﷻ. What was he given there? Salah. And what constitutes most of salah? Standing and reciting Quran. The most spiritual experience dictated by revelation is engagement with revelation itself in prayer.

When salah becomes empty—just movements without meaning—people search elsewhere for “real dhikr.” Rather than debating whether alternative spiritual practices align with Sunnah, we should recognize why they exist: there’s a spiritual void, and people haven’t been shown how to fill it properly. Muslims have underestimated the spiritual power of the Quran. It does purify. It has that power.

Once this purification and cleansing happens, the next stage follows.

Stage Three: Teaching the Book

Wa yu’allimuhumul kitab (وَيُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتَابَ)—“and he teaches them the Book.” Notice the wa (and)—this conjunction can indicate progression, not just simultaneity. First this, then this, then this.

Teaching the law means explaining: this is halal, this is haram, this is what you must do, this is what you must avoid. Here’s the framework for financial transactions, business conduct, marriage, spousal relations.

But why would people care about the law if they haven’t been cleansed internally? They wouldn’t. Without inner purification, people remain unconvinced of the benefits of following Shariah. It seems too restrictive. They question whether it suits modern lifestyles. They argue for “broader interpretations” suitable for different times.

You can construct elaborate justifications, but if tazkiyah hasn’t occurred, something is missing inside. When Quran is genuinely engaged with—even a little—debates about specific rulings become unnecessary. People naturally let go of harmful practices because Quran has affected them. Revelation possesses this power. If it could transform someone as dangerous as Umar (RA) before Islam, we shouldn’t underestimate its power to change people. This is among its miracles: it changes people.

Stage Four: Teaching Wisdom

Wal hikmah (وَالْحِكْمَةَ)—“and wisdom.” What is wisdom? Many think of wisdom as someone who posts profound statements on social media. That’s not wisdom in Islamic understanding.

In the Arabic language and Islamic tradition, hikmah is defined as beneficial knowledge combined with acting upon it. Wisdom can be as straightforward as understanding that fire burns and therefore not touching it. Someone who knows fire burns has knowledge. Someone who knows fire burns and doesn’t touch it has wisdom.

You can possess knowledge without wisdom—knowing right from wrong yet still choosing wrong. Wisdom includes the practical dimension: learning the law and then learning how to live by it, how to uphold it. When Sahaba (RA) made mistakes, they would come to the Prophet ﷺ, and he would teach them wisdom: this is the istighfar you make, this is how you avoid repeating this error. That’s wisdom. This isn’t abstract philosophy—it’s tarbiyah, practical spiritual cultivation.

The Complete Formula

These four elements—reciting revelation, purification, teaching the Book and teaching wisdom—constitute the Prophet’s formula ﷺ given by Allah ﷻ to transform people. This is what turns a person around. This formula turned the Makkans around, some of the most hardened people imaginable.

Today, we’ve made progress in yu’allimuhumul kitab—teaching the Book. In this information age, religious knowledge has become more accessible than ever before in human history. You can take more courses, read more books, engage in deeper discussions about Islam—even in translation—than ever before. Someone can discuss the four schools of thought in detail without knowing Arabic because they’ve studied translated works and scholarly research.

But the other three elements—the two before and one after—we haven’t developed systematic approaches for these. We lack programs for them. We need to develop frameworks that aren’t necessarily global but very local: two, three, four friends gathering together. They have someone knowledgeable among them who teaches. They learn Quran together, memorize together. Their ideas are being cleansed together. They help each other maintain spiritual awareness. They learn the Book and then encourage each other to act rightly, make correct decisions together.

This happens through community—people gathering and encouraging each other toward good. That’s why the Sahaba (RA) remained close to the Prophet ﷺ, allowing him to implement this methodology fully.

From Misguidance to Guidance

The ayah concludes with a profound observation: wa in kanu min qablu lafi dalalin mubin (وَإِن كَانُوا مِن قَبْلُ لَفِي ضَلَالٍ مُّبِينٍ)—“although they were previously in clear, open misguidance.”

These same people the Prophet ﷺ transformed were once completely lost. Before their transformation, if you had seen them, you would have written them off. You would never have imagined: this person will become something remarkable. For an extended period, they were way out there. You would have seen no potential in them whatsoever.

Yet Allah ﷻ turned them around.

This teaches us that if this formula is followed, Allah ﷻ can transform anyone He wills. That’s not our doing. We don’t change anybody. But when we commit to this formula, Allah ﷻ will, by means of it, turn around people we wouldn’t imagine, people we couldn’t predict. The transformation isn’t in our hands—we simply provide the means, and Allah ﷻ grants the transformation.

Ayah 3 · linked video: Al-Jumu’ah (Ayah 3)

Surah Al-Jumuah, Ayah 3

وَءَاخَرِينَ مِنْهُمْ لَمَّا يَلْحَقُوا۟ بِهِمْ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

This ayah speaks directly to us. Allah ﷻ declares that this divine message extends beyond the original recipients—beyond the unlettered Arabs of Makkah. The phrase وَءَاخَرِينَ مِنْهُمْ (wa akhareen minhum) means “and others among them” or “others from them.”

When the Ansar first encountered this ayah, they recognized themselves in it. They understood: “This is about us—we’re not the Makkans.” Yet Allah ﷻ did not specify “the people of Madinah” or name particular villages and tribes. He simply says “others beyond those original ones.” This universality is deliberate and profound.

The implications of this wording are breathtaking. Every Muslim who came after the Companions is included in these “others.” You and I are among the Akhareen minhum. When Islam reached Persia and countless Persians embraced the faith, they became the Akhareen minhum. When the message arrived in Europe, those believers joined this category. When Islam spread to India, they too were encompassed by these words.

This declaration in Surah Al-Jumuah carries particular significance in its historical context. For the Jews of Arabia, hearing that revelation came to the Ummiyeen—the unlettered ones—was deeply challenging. Their objection had always been that such people were unworthy of receiving divine guidance. Moreover, their tradition held that revelation belonged exclusively to their ethnic group, a legacy that could not be exported or shared with others. They believed: “This is ours. We own it.”

Allah ﷻ categorically rejects this notion. He establishes that others will embrace this message—people who haven’t even joined the Companions yet. The phrase لَمَّا يَلْحَقُوا۟ بِهِمْ (lamma yalhaqoo bihim) means “they have not yet joined them.” This “not yet” carries immense meaning. At the time of revelation, countless generations of believers had not yet come into existence.

The ayah concludes by affirming that He is Al-Azeez (the Ultimate Authority) and Al-Hakeem (the All-Wise). These divine names underscore that Allah ﷻ has decreed this universal spread of His message with perfect wisdom and unquestionable authority.

Allah ﷻ was already announcing that the Ummah would be international and multicultural. So many different kinds of people who hadn’t yet joined the Companions would eventually do so across generations and continents.

The beauty of يَلْحَقُوا۟ بِهِمْ (yalhaqoo bihim)—“joining them”—extends beyond this worldly life. Yes, we join the Companions through our shared faith in Islam. But there’s another dimension: one day, by Allah’s permission, we will join them in Jannah. Many of the Companions have already reached Paradise. When we, through Allah’s mercy, arrive there, we will be reunited with them—joining them in the ultimate sense.

This ayah encompasses all those who have not yet joined the Companions in faith, and by extension, those who will join them in eternal reward. He is the Ultimate Authority, the All-Wise.

Ayah 4 · linked video: Al-Jumu’ah (Ayah 4)

Ayah 4

ذَٰلِكَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ

“That is the favor of Allah. He gives it to whoever He wants. And Allah is the owner of the ultimate favor.”

This ayah serves as a powerful reminder about the nature of divine revelation itself. When Allah ﷻ says “that is the favor of Allah,” He is pointing to something profound about how revelation comes to humanity. It doesn’t come to everyone—it comes to select individuals whom Allah ﷻ chooses as His messengers. This is an essential response to those who question why revelation came to Muhammad ﷺ specifically. The answer is simple yet profound: revelation is Allah’s favor, and He grants His favor to whomever He wills.

Understanding Al-Hikmah

Before we explore the deeper implications of this ayah, we need to understand something crucial about the word الحكمة (al-hikmah) that appears in the previous ayah. Imam Malik (RA) provided a profound insight when he said that hikmah refers to the Sunnah. When Allah ﷻ mentions that the Prophet ﷺ teaches both the Book and the wisdom, He is distinguishing between the Quran and the Sunnah. The Book is the Quran itself, while hikmah represents the practical teachings—the lived application and demonstration of those divine principles through the Prophet’s ﷺ life and guidance.

This understanding is completely aligned with the deeper linguistic study of the word hikmah. When scholars examine this term on its own merits, they arrive at the same conclusion: hikmah represents the practical dimension of divine guidance. The Companions (RA) captured this meaning beautifully in simple yet profound explanations that reveal layers of depth upon closer examination.

The Democratization of Religious Knowledge

What, then, is the favor of Allah ﷻ that this ayah refers to? It’s that the Messenger ﷺ is transforming an entire nation into people who are educated in the Book. An entire ummah is being spiritually cleansed, with the ayat recited to them regularly. This represents something revolutionary in the history of religious communities.

Consider the greatest source of corruption in religious traditions throughout history. When does manipulation become possible? When does religion turn into an industry of social and economic control? When fanatics can incite people like sheep for their own gains? This happens when religious knowledge becomes the exclusive domain of a select few, while the masses depend entirely on their authority. When a small group holds all religious knowledge and everyone else simply accepts their pronouncements—“he said it, so it must be right”—that’s when manipulation becomes easy.

Islam introduced something fundamentally different. Yes, there will always be fuqaha (jurists), mujtahidun (scholars capable of independent reasoning), and ulama (learned scholars) of high caliber. The ummah needs and will always have such specialists. But here’s what makes Islam unique: the average Muslim is educated in the Quran itself. They know its contents. They understand its priorities. And crucially, they can recognize distortion when they hear it. They cannot be easily manipulated because they have direct access to the primary source of their faith.

Historical Context and Contrast

This represents a teaching that did not exist in previous religious communities in the same way. For the Jewish community, the main obstacle to accepting Islam was often the rabbis—the religious authorities who held exclusive knowledge. For the Christian community, it was the ministers and clergy who knew more than everyone else. Allah ﷻ even tells us that some of these religious leaders recognized the truth of the Quran’s message—they saw it as clearly as they recognized their own children. Yet they couldn’t bear to tell their people, and their followers couldn’t see it for themselves because they lacked direct education in revelation.

If those communities had been educated in their own scriptures at a mass level, they would have been able to recognize revelation for what it is. Most Christians, for example, don’t know the Bible comprehensively. They know select passages heard during Sunday services, but not the whole text. This pattern exists across faith communities where religious knowledge remains concentrated among an elite class.

Maintaining the Quran Connection

Islam was meant to be different—not just in its theological teachings, but in its social structure. The minimum standard for the average Muslim was to be versed in the Quran. Every Muslim was supposed to have exposure to the Quran and engagement with its teachings.

Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA) understood this principle deeply. During his khilafah, he would personally check whether every masjid and every group of Muslims had a halaqah (study circle) for Quran ongoing. He saw this as an essential component of maintaining good citizenship in the Muslim ummah. To preserve the integrity of the community, Quran education had to continue without interruption. It wasn’t a luxury or an optional extra—it was foundational to the health of the entire society.

The Ultimate Favor

The ayah concludes by reminding us: “that is the favor of Allah. He gives it to whoever He wants. And Allah is the owner of the ultimate favor.” This isn’t just about the Prophet ﷺ receiving revelation. It’s about an entire system of guidance that keeps a community connected to divine wisdom, that prevents the concentration of religious authority from becoming a tool of manipulation, and that empowers every believer with direct access to their Lord’s words.

When we understand this favor—this gift of widespread Quranic literacy and engagement—we begin to appreciate why maintaining our relationship with the Quran isn’t just about personal spirituality. It’s about preserving the very structure that keeps our faith authentic, our community healthy, and our connection to Allah ﷻ unmediated by human gatekeepers who might distort the message for their own ends.

Ayah 5 · linked video: Al-Jumu’ah (Ayah 5)

Surah Al-Jumuah: Ayah 5

مَثَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ حُمِّلُوا۟ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَحْمِلُوهَا كَمَثَلِ ٱلْحِمَارِ يَحْمِلُ أَسْفَارًۢا ۚ بِئْسَ مَثَلُ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّٰلِمِينَ

The example of those who were entrusted with the Torah and then did not take it on is like that of a donkey who carries volumes [of books]. Wretched is the example of the people who deny the signs of Allah ﷻ. And Allah ﷻ does not guide the wrongdoing people.

The Weight of Responsibility

Allah ﷻ begins this ayah by describing those who were given the Torah: الَّذِينَ حُمِّلُوا التَّوْرَاةَ (alladhīna ḥummilū at-tawrāt). The word حُمِّلُوا comes from the root ح م ل (ḥ-m-l), which means to carry or bear a burden. This verb appears in the passive form of the second pattern (تَحْمِيل - taḥmīl), which intensifies the meaning. They weren’t simply handed the Torah—they were entrusted with its enormous weight and responsibility. The Torah wasn’t a light burden; it was a major, major responsibility requiring dedication and commitment.

The beauty of this construction is what follows: ثُمَّ لَمْ يَحْمِلُوهَا (thumma lam yaḥmilūhā) - “then they did not carry it.” Notice that Allah ﷻ shifts from the intensive form (taḥmīl) back to the simple form (ḥaml). They were given this massive responsibility—yet they didn’t even fulfill the basic, minimal level of it. They didn’t even carry a little bit of what they were supposed to uphold. This linguistic shift emphasizes the depth of their failure: given everything, they delivered nothing.

Understanding the Example

The ayah continues: كَمَثَلِ الْحِمَارِ (ka-mathali al-ḥimār) - “like the example of a donkey.” In Arabic rhetoric, when you want to give an example, you can use the word مَثَل (mathal - example) or the particle كَ (ka - like). The ayah employs both: “their example is like the example of…” This construction creates what Arabic rhetoricians call تَبْعِيد (tab’īd) - distancing. It’s as if Allah ﷻ is saying these people are so far removed from what they should be that even comparing them to a donkey is generous. They’re remotely, distantly similar to this creature, though in reality they’re even worse.

The donkey يَحْمِلُ أَسْفَارًا (yaḥmilu asfārā) - “carries volumes of books.” The word أَسْفَار (asfār) comes from سَفَر (safar), which means journey or travel. Why are books called asfār? Because when you read, your eyes and fingers must journey through the text, navigating line by line by line. If it’s a lengthy journey through a text, it’s called safar—hence volumes of books are asfār because you’re literally traveling through knowledge.

But here’s the devastating point: the donkey has all these books loaded on its back, yet none of that knowledge enters it. The books remain external burdens, nothing more. There’s no internalization, no understanding, no transformation. The donkey wishes it didn’t have to carry this weight at all. This is the relationship these people developed with their scripture—it became a nuisance rather than guidance, a burden to be discarded rather than a treasure to be cherished.

Who Is This Ayah Addressing?

This is where we must be extremely careful. Allah ﷻ doesn’t say “the Jews” or even “Banī Isrāīl” in this ayah. He specifically restricts this description to an act: there were people burdened with the responsibility of revelation—in that case, the Torah—and those people didn’t carry that responsibility. The criticism is directed at a specific behavior, not at an entire people or nation.

Banī Isrāīl is not a curse word. It’s not a derogatory term. It’s an honorable name that includes the name of a prophet—Ya’qub (AS), also known as Israel. The Children of Israel received more prophets than any other nation. Yes, they had failures, but who are we to judge? Every nation has had its share of mistakes and shortcomings. This doesn’t negate the righteous among them whom Allah ﷻ compliments throughout the Quran.

Consider that Allah ﷻ speaks of the رَبَّانِيُّونَ (rabbāniyyūn) and الأَحْبَار (al-aḥbār) - the rabbis and scholars who fought alongside prophets. These were people of deep faith and commitment. Our modern political situation has unfortunately led many Muslims to become casually anti-Semitic, and this is a serious violation of our deen. We’re supposed to learn lessons from those who made mistakes in the past, not use those mistakes as justification for prejudice.

Why Does Allah ﷻ Tell Us Their History?

When Allah ﷻ addresses the Jewish tribes of Madinah in the Quran, He says: يَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتِيَ الَّتِي أَنْعَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ (O Children of Israel, remember My favor which I bestowed upon you). He mentions how He shaded them with clouds, provided them with manna and quail, and delivered them from Pharaoh. But here’s the interesting part: Allah ﷻ wasn’t addressing the people He had shaded with clouds. That generation had long passed. The Jewish tribes of Madinah—Banū Qurayzah, Banū Qaynuqā’, Banū Naḍīr—they never experienced those miracles firsthand.

So why does Allah ﷻ bunch them together with their ancestors? The answer reveals something profound: there was a particular attitude among some Jews of Madinah that perfectly mirrored the attitude of their disobedient ancestors. Allah ﷻ draws a parallel between the criminal element among Banī Isrāīl at the time of the Prophet ﷺ and the criminal element at the time of Musa (AS). It’s not a blanket condemnation—it’s a specific warning: “You want to take pride in your history? Well, you’re acting exactly like the shameful moments of that history.”

There’s probably no other nation whose history is discussed more in the Quran than Banī Isrāīl. Why? Because one source of pride that prevented some of them from accepting the Prophet ﷺ was their historical legacy. “We are children of the Israelite tradition. We have prophets in our lineage. Why should we accept a gentile messenger, a non-Israelite prophet?” Allah ﷻ responds by teaching them lessons from their history that should humble that pride.

The Lesson for Muslims

Here’s what we need to understand: Muslims love our history. We constantly reference the golden age, the great scholars, the magnificent contributions to science and civilization. But we don’t talk nearly enough about the embarrassing moments, the shameful periods, the times when we failed to live up to our ideals. Until we accept the uncomfortable parts of our history and learn from them, we haven’t truly learned the lesson Allah ﷻ is teaching through the example of Banī Isrāīl.

Allah ﷻ is telling a nation proud of its history: “You have prophets in your past, yes, but you’ve also made plenty of mistakes. Learn from those mistakes.” That’s the exact same message being directed at us. We have 1,400 years of history, and plenty of it is deeply troubling. We need to acknowledge our failures instead of romanticizing the past, wishing we could return to some imagined perfect era when scholars walked around with books and everyone got along beautifully. No—we’ve had plenty of really sad, really difficult moments in our history, and we must confront them honestly.

Books Without Understanding

Consider the irony Allah ﷻ presents here: “How can Muhammad ﷺ be a legitimate prophet? He can’t even read!” Yet those making this objection had volumes upon volumes of books—أَسْفَار (asfār). Allah ﷻ responds with this devastating image: you have library shelves full of books, but what have you internalized? What has actually entered your hearts and transformed your behavior? The books are just burdens on your backs, like cargo on a donkey. You wish you didn’t have to carry them. There’s no real relationship with these texts except that they’re a nuisance.

The corrupt among them would simply change the difficult teachings because the truth became too heavy a burden. In Surat Al-Baqarah, Allah ﷻ describes how they نَبَذُوهُ وَرَاءَ ظُهُورِهِمْ (nabadhuhu warā’a ẓuhūrihim) - “threw it behind their backs.” And where are these books on the donkey? On its back. They threw the truth behind their backs as though they didn’t even know it was there.

Our Relationship With the Quran

What happens when there’s a significant population among us who claim loyalty to the Quran but know nothing of its actual teachings? Who don’t speak genuinely about what it says? I recently witnessed a program where an ayah was being translated for a public event: هَٰذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُونِ (This is your ummah, one ummah, and I am your Lord, so worship Me). The translation displayed read: “Your community is one community, and I am your Lord.”

The word أُمَّة (ummah) was reduced to “community”—as if it means your local neighborhood or your national citizenship. The ayah was being used to suggest a kind of interfaith unity under “one nation under God.” But that’s not what the ayah means at all. In that particular context, ummah refers specifically to the Muslim faith, the unified body of believers. You cannot twist the meaning of the Quran to fit a political agenda or to make non-Muslims feel comfortable.

I’m not saying we shouldn’t have programs about being responsible citizens or building bridges with our neighbors—we absolutely should. But don’t misrepresent the Book to do it. There are plenty of other ayat that genuinely support cooperation and good relations. Take the ayah: وَتَعَاوَنُوا عَلَى الْبِرِّ وَالتَّقْوَىٰ (Cooperate in righteousness and piety). Notice there’s no restriction on who you can cooperate with—only what you can cooperate in. If Muslims are engaged in wrong, you’re not obligated to join them. If non-Muslims are doing good, you can absolutely work alongside them. Use verses that actually support your point without distorting their meaning.

This casual misrepresentation of the Quran is exactly the problem this ayah addresses. When people become distant from the Book as a culture, when they’re comfortable with that distance, they start making assumptions about what it says. They put the Quran on the back shelf, in the car’s dashboard, and then casually cite it in conversation without any real knowledge: “The Quran says if you pray Fajr, your day will be blessed. If you pray Dhuhr, you’ll get a raise. Forward this to twenty people or Allah ﷻ will hold you accountable!” This is the behavior of people who have books on their backs but nothing in their hearts.

The Severity of the Crime

The ayah concludes with a devastating assessment: بِئْسَ مَثَلُ الْقَوْمِ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ (How terrible is the example of the people who deny the signs of Allah ﷻ). Notice the word كَذَّبُوا (kadhdhabū) - they lied against, they denied. How is ignoring revelation the same as denying it? Because when you have the truth in your possession and you don’t care to learn it, when you don’t let it transform your life, you are effectively denying it. You’re lying against the ayat of Allah ﷻ through your actions, even if not through your words.

This is a form of ظُلْم (ẓulm) - wrongdoing. What makes it such a grave wrong? These were people given revelation and divine favor more than any other nation. They were supposed to represent the teachings of Allah ﷻ so the world could see what guidance looks like. When they failed to live up to that responsibility, they became the ultimate deniers of revelation—not because they explicitly rejected it, but because they didn’t let it penetrate their hearts and shape their lives.

The principle is clear: the people most exposed to the truth become most responsible for it. If we Muslims claim to be believers in this Book, if we have more access to its teachings than anyone else, then we bear the greatest responsibility. If we don’t represent its values, we become the most accountable for that failure.

The ayah ends: وَاللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ (And Allah ﷻ does not guide the wrongdoing people). When a people wrong themselves by failing to honor the revelation they’ve been given, Allah ﷻ withdraws His guidance from them. This is the natural consequence of refusing to carry the responsibility you’ve been entrusted with.

Before we concern ourselves with questions about who’s going to Paradise and who’s going to Hell, before we worry about the spiritual fate of others, we need to examine ourselves. Are we living up to the Book we claim to follow? Have we internalized its teachings or are they just decorative volumes on our shelves? When we study what Allah ﷻ says about Banī Isrāīl and honestly apply it to ourselves, the application becomes unavoidable—and sometimes, it’s even more relevant to us than it was to them. We cannot afford to be donkeys carrying books, burdened by a revelation we’ve never truly embraced.

Ayah 6 · linked video: Al-Jumu’ah (Ayah 6)

Ayah 6

قُلْ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوٓا۟ إِن زَعَمْتُمْ أَنَّكُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ لِلَّهِ مِن دُونِ ٱلنَّاسِ فَتَمَنَّوُا۟ ٱلْمَوْتَ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

Allah ﷻ directs the Prophet ﷺ to challenge the Jewish community with a powerful test of their claim to exclusive divine favor. The phrase الَّذِينَ هَادُوا (alladhina hadu) refers to those who practice Judaism—literally, those who have engaged themselves in this faith tradition.

The Claim of Exclusive Friendship with Allah

The ayah addresses their fundamental assertion: that they are أَوْلِيَآءُ لِلَّهِ مِن دُونِ ٱلنَّاسِ (awliyaa lillahi min dooninnaas)—the close friends, the protected friends, the specially chosen allies of Allah ﷻ, set apart from all other people. This confident assumption forms the core of their religious identity, the belief that their covenant with Allah ﷻ guarantees them salvation regardless of their deeds.

The Logic of the Challenge

Allah ﷻ presents them with an irrefutable logical test: فَتَمَنَّوُا۟ ٱلْمَوْتَ (fatammannawul mawt)—then wish for death. This is a stunning challenge because it exposes the contradiction between their claim and their behavior. If someone truly has a guaranteed place in Jannah, why would they want to remain in this world? Consider what this life entails: paying bills, aging, illness, the daily needs of eating and hygiene, the physical discomfort of heat and cold. Why would anyone willingly endure the trials and limitations of this temporary existence if they had absolute certainty of eternal bliss waiting for them?

The comparison is vivid: imagine living in a cramped, uncomfortable apartment when you’ve received official confirmation that you’ve won a magnificent mansion just two blocks away. The keys are ready, the deed bears your name, and they’re waiting for you to move in. Would you hesitate even a moment? Would you choose to stay in that cramped space? Of course not. You would immediately go claim what’s rightfully yours.

Yet we find that those who claim guaranteed salvation never actually pray for death. They don’t wish for it. They don’t ask Allah ﷻ to take them quickly to their promised reward. This silence reveals the truth—they don’t truly believe their own claim.

The Islamic Perspective on Death

Interestingly, Muslims have been taught to approach death very differently. We have been given a dua that asks Allah ﷻ to take us among the righteous. We don’t claim absolute certainty of salvation; instead, we seek to be counted among those who have earned Allah’s pleasure through their faith and deeds. This humility stands in stark contrast to the arrogant certainty being challenged in this ayah.

A Warning for Muslims

This challenge isn’t merely historical—it carries a profound warning for Muslim communities as well. A significant portion of the Ummah has fallen into a similar trap of false confidence. How often do we hear people say, “Why would Allah ﷻ send us to Hell? We said لا إله إلا الله (la ilaha illallah).” They point to the hadith where the Prophet ﷺ said that whoever declares la ilaha illallah enters Jannah and consider the matter closed.

But this represents a dangerous misunderstanding. Yes, the Prophet ﷺ said these words, but what else did he say in that same conversation? How did he explain this statement? How did the companion who heard it directly understand and apply it? Did that companion interpret it as a license to sin freely, thinking their declaration had already secured their place in Paradise? Of course not. The companions (RA) understood these teachings within their complete context—they knew the requirements of faith, the necessity of righteous deeds, the importance of sincerity, and the reality of accountability.

When someone extracts a single hadith and ignores everything else the Quran and Sunnah teach about faith, deeds, sincerity and accountability, they’re engaging in the same selective approach that Allah ﷻ criticizes in other communities. The Quran describes those who believe in some parts of the Book while ignoring others, who accept the aspects of divine law that suit them while rejecting what challenges them. Allah ﷻ speaks of those who تَفَرَّقُوا (farraqoo)—who tore the Quran into pieces, clinging to whatever fragment serves their desires while discarding the rest.

This is the danger of cherry-picking religious texts—taking one piece and claiming it’s all you need while ignoring the comprehensive guidance that provides proper understanding and balance.

The Final Test: Truth or Falsehood

The ayah concludes with إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (in kuntum sadiqeen)—if you are truthful. This phrase exposes the claim for what it is: either truth or falsehood, with no middle ground. If they truly believed they were guaranteed Jannah, their entire relationship with death would be different. Their prayers, their aspirations, their daily choices would all reflect an eagerness to leave this temporary world for the eternal reward they claim awaits them.

The silence that follows this challenge speaks volumes. It reveals that deep within, they know their claim is hollow. They love this world too much, they fear death too greatly, and they harbor too many doubts to actually wish for what they claim is their certain destiny. This test of sincerity remains relevant across time, challenging anyone who claims guaranteed salvation to examine whether their actions align with their assertions.

Ayah 7 · linked video: Al-Jumu’ah (Ayah 7)

Ayah 7

وَلَا يَتَمَنَّوْنَهُۥٓ أَبَدًۢا بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلظَّٰلِمِينَ

“But they will never wish for it, ever, because of what their hands have put forth. And Allah ﷻ is Knowing of the wrongdoers.”

The ayah tells us they will never wish for death—not now, not ever. The reason is powerfully simple: بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ (bima qaddamat aydeehim)—because of what their hands have already sent forward. This phrase captures a profound reality about human nature and investment. The things they’ve done, the choices they’ve made, the priorities they’ve set—all of these have created a psychological state where death is the last thing they want.

The Psychology of Investment

When someone makes an investment, they don’t just put money somewhere and forget about it. They anticipate. They wait. They check on it. They think about the day when the returns will come—the payday, the harvest, the reward. If all your investments are tied to this world, if everything you’ve worked for exists only here, then why would you ever want to leave? Your entire focus becomes waiting for those worldly returns to materialize.

This is the trap the ayah describes. These people have invested everything in the dunya—their time, energy, wealth and hopes—and they’re waiting for the payoff. Death would mean leaving before collecting on those investments, and that’s unthinkable to them.

The Balanced Investment Strategy of Islam

Our deen doesn’t ask us to choose between dunya and Akhirah. Allah ﷻ doesn’t say ignore one for the other. Instead, Islam teaches us to combine both investments intelligently. When you earn a halal income, you’re not just making money for this life—you’re investing in both worlds simultaneously. The act of earning halal is itself an act of worship that benefits you here and hereafter.

Consider the example of sadaqah (charity). It’s one of the most beautiful investments because it serves both realms. The Prophet ﷺ promised us that wealth never decreases when you give charity. This isn’t just spiritual comfort—it’s a guarantee. When you give sadaqah, Allah ﷻ will replace it for you in this world. That’s your dunya return, guaranteed.

The Mathematics of Charity

But the Akhirah investment is where the real numbers become staggering. You’ve deposited your wealth into an account that pays a minimum of 700 times your investment. The Quran describes planting a single seed that produces an ear of grain with a hundred seeds. Now multiply that by seven ears—that’s 700 seeds. But the description doesn’t stop there. Each of those 700 seeds, when planted, produces another hundred seeds across seven ears. The multiplication continues infinitely.

And then Allah ﷻ adds: وَاللَّهُ يُضَاعِفُ لِمَن يَشَاءُ—“And Allah multiplies for whom He wills” (2:261). This is where human calculation breaks down. We can imagine infinity in mathematical terms, but Allah’s ﷻ concept of multiplication transcends our comprehension entirely. He’s saying, “Everything I just described—the 700, the exponential growth—that’s just what you can wrap your mind around. I have a multiplication scheme beyond your calculus.”

The Security of Your Investment

When we save in this world, our savings face constant threats. Your bank account is only insured up to a certain amount. Gold can be stolen. Real estate can flood or shake apart in an earthquake. Every worldly investment carries risk.

But when you deposit in your Akhirah account, it’s absolutely secure—with one critical exception. There’s only one threat to your Akhirah savings: showing off or bringing up your charity to people in a way that hurts them. This is called mann—reminding others of your favor. If you ever say to someone, “I gave that masjid $10,000, what have you people done with it?”—it’s gone. The entire investment is wiped out. You stood up in front of people, made it known, hurt feelings with your charity—you get nothing in the Akhirah.

This is why if you’re going to write a big check, you must be quiet about it. No expectations. No recognition. No announcements. Just you and Allah ﷻ, and that’s it. Otherwise, you could have at least bought something enjoyable for this world—a big screen TV, a vacation, anything. Instead, you end up with no dunya benefit and no Akhirah reward. Absolutely nothing.

Divine Knowledge of Wrongdoing

The ayah concludes with a sobering reminder: وَاللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلظَّٰلِمِينَ—“And Allah is Knowing of the wrongdoers.” Allah ﷻ has complete knowledge of every act of injustice, every misplaced priority, every investment made in the wrong direction. He knows those who wrong themselves by investing only in this temporary world, and He knows those who nullify their good deeds through showing off or hurting others.

This divine knowledge should inspire both caution and hope—caution in how we invest our lives, and hope that when we invest sincerely for His sake, He ﷻ knows that too, and will never let it go to waste.

Ayah 8 · linked video: Al-Jumu’ah (Ayah 8)

Ayah 8

قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلْمَوْتَ ٱلَّذِى تَفِرُّونَ مِنْهُ فَإِنَّهُۥ مُلَـٰقِيكُمْ ۖ ثُمَّ تُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰ عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

Tell them: No doubt, the death that you people are running away from—it is absolutely going to meet you. The Arabic construction here uses مُلَاقِيكُمْ (mulāqīkum), an irregular مُفَاعَلَة (mufāʿalah) form that carries the sense of an ongoing encounter. It’s not just “it will meet you” in some distant future, but rather “it is meeting you”—almost as if death is already on its way, bound to meet you without escape.

Then you will all be returned, taken back to the Knower of the unseen and the seen, and He will thoroughly inform you of all the things that you used to do.

Allah ﷻ dedicates three ayahs in this passage to address death and the Bani Israel. When they failed to truly carry their Book—when the Torah became something on their backs rather than in their hearts—a specific spiritual consequence followed: they began to wish to live forever. They developed an intense desire to avoid death, to run from it. Allah ﷻ declares that no matter how much they flee, they will face it, and they will be brought before Him for judgment.

This connection is profound. Allah ﷻ establishes a relationship between disconnection from revelation and the development of fear of death. When people lose their genuine tie to divine guidance, when scripture becomes a burden carried rather than light internalized, death transforms from a meeting with Allah ﷻ into something terrifying to escape.

The Prophet ﷺ warned that the Ummah would experience weakness, that other nations would consume from us like wolves attacking prey. The Companions (RA) asked whether Muslims would be few in number on that day. He ﷺ responded that numbers wouldn’t be the issue—weakness would be the problem. When asked how to alleviate this weakness, he ﷺ prescribed two specific remedies: remember death frequently and recite the Quran. These two practices would remove weakness from the believers.

Consider how perfectly this prophetic guidance mirrors the issues raised in Surah Al-Jumuah. What were the two interconnected problems mentioned regarding the Jews? First, they had a superficial relationship with their Book—it sat on their backs like a burden. Second, they were running from death, desperate to avoid it. The Prophet ﷺ warned us that we would face similar weakness, saying that we would follow the previous nations so closely that we’d be like one slipper compared to another.

The cure, then, is the opposite of the disease. Where there is disconnection from revelation, reconnect through recitation of the Quran. Where there is fear and avoidance of death, face it directly through frequent remembrance of death. These two practices together restore strength to the believer, transforming both our relationship with Allah’s ﷻ words and our understanding of our ultimate return to Him.

Ayah 11 · linked video: Al-Jumu’ah (Ayah 11)

Surah Al-Jumuah: Ayah 11

Ayah 11

وَإِذَا رَأَوْا تِجَـٰرَةً أَوْ لَهْوًا ٱنفَضُّوٓا۟ إِلَيْهَا وَتَرَكُوكَ قَآئِمًا ۚ قُلْ مَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهْوِ وَمِنَ ٱلتِّجَـٰرَةِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ

And when they saw business or entertainment, they rushed towards it and left you standing. Say, “What Allah ﷻ has is better than entertainment and better than business. And Allah ﷻ is the best of providers.”

This final ayah of Surah Al-Jumuah addresses a serious incident that occurred during the early days of establishing Friday prayer in Madinah. The Muslim community at that time consisted of companions who had been with the Prophet ﷺ for many years, newer Muslims and young people still learning the fundamentals of their faith. The specific rulings about Friday prayer—such as the prohibition of talking during the khutbah or leaving before its completion—had not yet been fully internalized by the community. This was still the early period of implementing the Friday congregational prayer.

The Incident: When a Trade Caravan Arrived

During one of these Friday sermons, a trade caravan arrived in Madinah. To understand the significance of this event, we must recognize what trade caravans represented in that era. These were not simple merchant visits but major commercial events—similar to modern conventions and expos that draw thousands of attendees. Whether it’s a technology expo, an automotive show or any major trade event today, these gatherings create excitement and draw crowds. In the Arabian context, when a caravan arrived from distant lands like Iraq, it brought exotic goods, opportunities for trade and considerable commotion.

The arrival of such a caravan was accompanied by all the fanfare and activity we might associate with a modern convention—music, noise, crowds gathering and general excitement. As the Friday prayer was taking place, this caravan settled near the masjid, creating a distraction that would test the commitment of those attending the sermon.

Two Types of People Drawn Away

Allah ﷻ reveals in this ayah: وَإِذَا رَأَوْا تِجَـٰرَةً أَوْ لَهْوًا - “When they saw business or entertainment.” The order of these two words is deliberate and meaningful. Business (تِجَـٰرَة - tijarah) is mentioned first because the initial wave of people who left were businesspeople. These individuals saw the caravan as an opportunity for networking, making sales and establishing commercial connections. For them, this was a professional opportunity they felt they couldn’t miss.

However, once ten or twelve people stood up and began congregating near the caravan, a second type of person was drawn away. These were individuals without any particular business purpose—those simply looking for entertainment or distraction. When they saw others gathering, curiosity got the better of them. “What’s happening over there? Why are people gathering?” And so they followed, not for business but purely for amusement.

This is why Allah ﷻ mentions both reasons in this specific order: business drew the first group, and entertainment drew the second.

The Severity of Breaking Away: The Word Infaddu

The Arabic verb used here is particularly powerful: ٱنفَضُّوٓا۟ (infaddu). This word carries a much heavier meaning than simply “they left” or “they went.” The root of this word relates to breaking glass—when glass shatters, the pieces scatter and cannot be reassembled. Allah ﷻ uses this same linguistic form in Surah Ali Imran when He tells the Prophet ﷺ that people would have “broken away” from him under certain circumstances.

When Allah ﷻ describes their departure using infaddu, He is indicating that they didn’t just step away momentarily—they shattered the gathering in a way that could not be recovered. The Friday congregation, once broken in this manner, was irreparably disrupted. What we learn from this choice of words is that disturbing the Jumuah gathering requires very little, but once people begin leaving in numbers, the damage to the congregation becomes permanent for that session.

It’s natural for one or two people to need to leave—perhaps to attend to a crying child or for an urgent matter. But when people begin departing in groups, it creates a cascading effect. It becomes deeply distracting not only to the speaker but to everyone trying to listen and benefit from the reminder.

They Left You Standing

وَتَرَكُوكَ قَآئِمًا - “And they left you standing.” They abandoned the Messenger of Allah ﷺ—the greatest teacher humanity has ever known—standing and delivering his sermon.

This becomes even more significant when we remember the beginning of this surah. Allah ﷻ described the Prophet’s mission through four essential elements: reciting the ayat to them, purifying them, teaching them the Book and teaching them wisdom. All four of these responsibilities converge and manifest in the Friday khutbah. During Jumuah, the Prophet ﷺ would recite Allah’s revelation, provide spiritual purification through his reminders, teach them something new from the Book and impart wisdom to guide their lives. The people sitting and listening were actively fulfilling their covenant with Allah ﷻ—they were receiving this comprehensive guidance.

The Friday khutbah, therefore, is not merely a weekly sermon. It is a pillar of the Prophet’s entire mission, a strategic means of executing the divine mandate given to him. This makes the khutbah one of the most sacred responsibilities in any Muslim community. It should be an exceptional presentation every single week, delivered with care, preparation and consciousness of its profound role in continuing the legacy of the Prophet ﷺ.

Unfortunately, this remains one of the most neglected responsibilities in many of our communities today. Resources are poured into beautiful architecture—imported chandeliers, Persian carpets, fine marble, expansive parking lots—but insufficient investment is made in the quality and preparation of the khutbah itself. Sometimes the sermon becomes more about organizational announcements than Allah’s Book, more about fundraising than spiritual nourishment, or more about political commentary than divine guidance. This represents a violation of the sacred trust that has been placed upon our communities.

What Allah Has Is Better

Allah ﷻ instructs His Messenger ﷺ: قُلْ مَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ خَيْرٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهْوِ وَمِنَ ٱلتِّجَـٰرَةِ - “Say, ‘What Allah ﷻ has is better than entertainment and better than business.’”

Notice the reversal in order. At the beginning of the ayah, business was mentioned before entertainment because that particular incident involved a trade caravan—businesspeople left first, followed by those seeking entertainment. However, when Allah ﷻ presents the solution and reminds people of what truly matters, entertainment is mentioned before business.

Why this shift? Because we’ve moved from describing a specific historical incident to addressing the universal, ongoing problem that all Muslims face. While some people struggle with work commitments preventing them from attending Jumuah, everyone—without exception—faces the temptation of entertainment and distraction. Entertainment is the more common, more widespread culprit. Therefore, in addressing the eternal lesson, Allah ﷻ mentions entertainment first because it represents the broader challenge.

The Profound Wisdom: “What Allah Has”

Here lies a remarkable piece of divine wisdom. Those companions left behind the best speaker who ever lived—the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ himself. His khutbah was perfect in every way. Yet Allah ﷻ didn’t say, “What the Prophet has is better” or “The Prophet’s words are better than your entertainment.” Instead, He said, مَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ - “What Allah ﷻ has is better.”

Why this phrasing? Because the Prophet ﷺ would not always be present to deliver the khutbah. There would come generations where the speaker might be less eloquent, where the Arabic might not be perfect, where the delivery might not be captivating. There might be a khatib whose tajweed needs improvement, who speaks in a language you don’t understand or whose presentation doesn’t inspire you the way you hoped.

The command to value what Allah ﷻ has transcends the quality of the human delivery. Even if you attend a khutbah delivered in a language you don’t speak, even if the speaker’s style doesn’t resonate with you, you are still fulfilling your obligation to Allah ﷻ. You’re not there for the entertainment value or even necessarily for the specific content you’ll absorb that day. You’re there because it is Jumuah, because it is your appointment with Allah ﷻ, because it is an act of obedience to Him.

This doesn’t mean we shouldn’t strive for excellence in our khutbahs—we absolutely should. Communities should work to ensure their Friday sermons are well-prepared, relevant and delivered effectively. But it does mean that the individual Muslim’s obligation to attend and to respect the sanctity of the gathering remains constant, regardless of the human element.

Allah Is the Best of Providers

وَٱللَّهُ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ - “And Allah ﷻ is the best of providers.”

What provision has Allah ﷻ been discussing throughout this surah? Not merely physical sustenance, but something far greater. He has been speaking about revelation, about the Prophet’s companionship and guidance, about the four elements of the prophetic mission: the ayat of the Quran, spiritual purification, teaching of the Book and imparting of wisdom.

These four gifts represent the ultimate provision—divine guidance itself. When we worry about missing a business opportunity or entertainment, we must remember that what we gain from Jumuah is infinitely more valuable. The guidance, knowledge and spiritual nourishment we receive is the provision that truly matters, the provision that will benefit us not just in this temporary life but in the eternal life to come.

Allah ﷻ is telling us that He is the best of all providers. The trade you might miss, the entertainment you might forego, the networking opportunity that passes—Allah ﷻ will provide for you in ways you cannot imagine. But the guidance you miss, the reminder you fail to receive, the connection with your Creator that you neglect—these represent losses that cannot be easily recovered.

This ayah stands as an eternal reminder to every Muslim community and every individual believer: guard your Friday prayer, honor the khutbah, respect the gathering and remember that what Allah ﷻ offers you in those moments is better than anything the world can provide. It is not just a weekly ritual but a continuation of the prophetic mission, a means of staying connected to divine guidance and a provision that surpasses all worldly provisions.
